'''Emerie''' is a female [[Illyrian]] who owns a [[Emerie's Shop|clothier shop]] in [[Windhaven Camp]]. She also helped revive the [[Valkyries]], a clan of female warriors, with [[Nesta Archeron]] and [[Gwyneth Berdara]].

## History
### Early Life
Emerie was born in Windhaven Camp, the daughter of [[Proteus]], a wealthy Illyrian who owned a clothing store, and an Illyrian female. She had an older brother who died ten years before she was born. Emerie's father was abusive and would beat his wife. One time he beat her so badly she couldn't recover and died. He made Emerie dig her mother's grave and told people she'd miscarried a baby, dying from blood loss. People believed him as he was charming and smart, a seemingly good male in front of others.

After the death of her mother, it's when her father started to beat Emerie, he beat her so badly, he once broke her back. He kept her in bed while she healed, telling people she was ill. On other occasions he would slam her head into walls, crunch her fingers in a door, abusing her until she blacked out. Emerie has always suspected her mother somehow shielded her from the abuse because the beatings started after her death.

At some point before the [[The War with Hybern|war]] with [[Hybern]], Emerie's abusive father clipped her wings following [[Clipping|outdated Illyrian customs]] for females, making her unable to fly. He never gave her any money or allowed her to make her own, he would also control her eating, never letting her eat more than what he deemed appropriate.

Proteus then died fighting during the war with Hybern, when Emerie received the news she was happy yet her family expected her to mourn him. She considered at this point telling people of the abuse she and her mother endured at his hands but ultimately decided not to because she thought they wouldn't believe her as they had turned a blind eye to her clipping. Emerie gained control over her father's shop, much to the displeasure of her uncle, his family, and the men of the camp, who stopped buying there. Since then, they have tried to persuade and threaten her to give up the store so they might have it and marry her off to a male.

### ''[[A Court of Frost and Starlight]]''
Cassian visits the store and buys all her winter gear, including gloves, boots, scarves, and coats that she has available. He tells Emerie that a big storm is coming and asks her to distribute the clothes to those most in need, as a gift from their [[High Lord]], [[Rhysand]].

Emerie informs Cassian that a lot of the other Illyrians don't like her. But she will make sure the items are delivered by sundown. She wishes him Happy [[Winter Solstice|Solstice]] before he leaves. He returns the sentiment and tells her to send word if she has trouble with the deliveries. She replies that she's sure she won't need to. Cassian can tell that Emerie will make the families take them, whether they wanted to or not.

### ''[[A Court of Silver Flames]]''
About six months after the Winter Solstice, Emerie meets Nesta whilst the latter was training in the [[Illyrian Mountains]]. The Archeron sister noticed her clipped wings, to which Emerie explained and Nesta offered her condolences for her wings. Nesta requested leather training clothes for winter, to which Emerie replied that she can inquire with her suppliers to get them. She then asked if the High Lord will pay for it since it's apparent she works for him, to which Nesta declines, saying reluctantly that she was cut off. Despite this, Emerie stated that she can pay later whenever she could, much to Nesta's gratitude.

(*to be continued*)

### ''[[w:c:crescentcity:House of Flame and Shadow (novel)|House of Flame and Shadow]]''
When Nesta uses [[the Mask]] to defeat a [[Middengard Wyrm]] she has a hard time breaking out of its hold so [[Azriel]] tries to help her snap out of it by mentioning all the people that love Nesta. He mentions Gwyn and Emerie along with Nesta's mate and her family and says they are waiting for her.

## Appearance
Emerie is a slender young female with dark hair in a simple braid, brown angular eyes, light brown skin and a narrow, angular face contrasting her full mouth. She is described as striking. She assumed to have some connection to the Dawn Court due to her appearance. Her wings have scars down the center tendons from being clipped. She wears a white apron and a simple brown dress when Cassian sees her at the store.

## Personality
Emerie is strong in a culture that demands she be weak. When her father dies, she insists that his store is ''hers'' and keeps it running. Emerie does not lower her eyes in the way Illyrian females are ordered and trained to do. Despite her [[Clipping|clipped]] wings, she holds Cassian's stare in a frank and unsettling manner. Cassian describes her as sharp, shrewd and unafraid. He also notes the fire in her words. Her posture is strong, with her back straight and chin upraised. Her grip is strong and unfaltering.

She is resilient, for it is her who carries [[Gwyneth Berdara|Gwyn]] up the mountain and finishes the [[Illyrian Blood Rite|Blood Rite]].

## Powers and Abilities
It is unknown if Emerie has any Illyrian trademark sort of power. However, it is assumed that she’s immortal due to her Illyrian heritage.

## Relationships
### [[Nesta Archeron]]
Nesta and Emerie become best friends.

### [[Gwyneth Berdara]]
Best friends. Helped renew the Valkyries again.

This '''unnamed female''' was an [[Illyrian]] from [[Windhaven Camp]]. She was [[Proteus]]' wife and had [[Emerie's Brother|a son]] and a daughter, [[Emerie]].

## History
### Early Life
She lived in Windhaven Camp, and was married to [[Proteus]], a wealthy Illyrian who owned [[Emerie's Shop|a clothing store]]. She had a son who died ten years before her second child was born, a daughter, [[Emerie]]. Her husband was abusive and would beat her. One time he beat her so badly she couldn't recover and died. He made Emerie dig her mother's grave and told people she'd miscarried a baby, dying from blood loss. People believed him as he was charming and smart, a seemingly good male in front of others.

After her death, it's when Proteus started to beat Emerie, he beat her so badly, he once broke her back. He kept her in bed while she healed, telling people she was ill. On other occasions he would slam her head into walls, crunch her fingers in a door, abusing her until she blacked out. Emerie has always suspected her mother somehow shielded her from the abuse because the beatings started after her death.

'''Enalius''' is a warrior-god and the first [[Illyrian]] who died defending [[Ramiel]] from the ancient enemies.

## History
At some point, Enalius drew a line in the dirt and held the ground against enemy hordes at the Pass on [[Ramiel]]. He died after defending the Pass for three days. He climbed with his guts hanging out to the sacred stone at the top and died there. The [[Blood Rite]] is done in his honor and the Pass was named "Pass of Enalius" after him.

## Trivia
* The name Enalius is possibly derived from Enyalius. In Greek mythology, [[Wikipedia:Enyalius|Enyalius]] or Enyalios is a son of [[Wikipedia:Ares|Ares]] who is often seen as the God of soldiers and warriors from Ares cult.
* [[Cassian]]'s fighting style is said to be similar to Enalius.

'''Eris Vanserra''' is the eldest son of [[Beron Vanserra]], the [[High Lord]] of the [[Autumn Court]], and the General of the Autumn Court's forces.

## History
[[Keir]], in an attempt to woo the Autumn Court into an alliance, offered his daughter [[Morrigan]]'s hand in marriage to Beron's eldest son, Eris. Morrigan decided to escape Autumn to lose her virginity before the wedding night with a bastard-born [[Illyrian]] named [[Cassian]], so that she would not have to marry Eris. When Eris hears of this, he publicly refuses to marry her, claiming that she had been "sullied by a bastard-born lesser faerie," thus ruining Keir and Beron's fragile alliance.

In response, Morrigan's family (father, Keir) left her on the Autumn Court border stripped naked, having been tortured by them, with a note nailed to her womb that announced that she was Eris' problem. Eris, however, while out with guards, saw her and demanded them not to touch her, knowing that she would be stuck in Autumn, in a life she would never want and already did not choose, if they did touch her, according to Autumn laws. They listened, albeit hesitantly, and he left her there in the woods, where she was later found by Azriel and taken to Velaris to live with the Inner Circle.

### ''[[A Court of Wings and Ruin]]''
Eris and his brothers happen upon [[Lucien Vanserra]] and [[Feyre Archeron]], who had just fled from the [[Spring Court]] and were traveling to the [[Night Court]] on foot until they regained their magic. Eris captures Feyre and places a gag and binds of fire on her, but Feyre notes that he isnt trying very hard, stating "I knew that these binds would burn--if Eris willed it." Feyre still evades him and kicks him in the balls. By then, Feyre and Lucien had managed to make it to the [[Winter Court]] borders and are then rescued by [[Azriel]] and Cassian. Eris and his brothers are overwhelmed and bested by the Illyrian warriors, but Feyre, however, decides to spare them. The Autumn Court sons flee back to their homeland relatively unscathed.

Later on, Eris allies himself with the Night Court, however slightly. Hungry for power and his father's throne, he strikes a deal with [[Rhysand]]. In exchange for his help in seducing Keir into an alliance with Rhysand against [[Hybern]], Eris was guaranteed Rhysand's support should he decide to overthrow his father and claim his father's throne. During the course of the novel, Beron's support of the resistance toward Hybern fluctuates, and it is considered by the Night Court to kill Beron and crown Eris, earning a more steadfast ally. During the High Lord's Meeting, a Dawn Court alchemist named Nuan (a friend of Lucien's who made him mechanical eye), is revealed to have created an antidote to faebane. Everyone agrees to use it during the war, including Eris. Beron gets angry at this and tell him that he will not use the antidote, Eris brushes him off and seems to be interested in it. Ultimately, Beron is forced to support Rhysand and aid the Night Court and their allies in the final battle against Hybern in the [[Mortal Lands]], due to [[Tamlin]] dragging him out by his neck and forcing him to fight with them, gaining the Courts another army to help fight the battle, as well as a makeshift one that Tamlin gathered after his army was killed in the insurrection in the Spring Court that took place due to Feyre. 

Eris also stated to Rhysand and Feyre that he refused to participate in the torture and death of [[Jesminda]], Lucien's [[Lesser Faeries|lesser Fae]] lover, that "it was the one thing I have ever denied my father." He says that when he refused, he was locked up and tortured and once he was finally released, he realized his father also planned on killing Lucien. Therefore, Eris chose to save Lucien's life by alerting Tamlin to his upcoming presence near the Spring Court border as he fled from three of their other brothers, on command of Beron to slaughter him. When Lucien reaches the border, Tamlin and Lucien kill two of the three brothers that try to attack and kill Lucien, as they had officially stepped over the border to Spring and were considered an unwanted threat that could be dealt with as Tamlin saw fit. The third brother runs home unscathed. Lucien defected to the Spring Court and is hailed emissary. 

Eris later lets it be known that he had actually ended his engagement with Morrigan because he knew she did not desire the union between them, not because he was disgusted at her union with Cassian, but he had to make a public show in front of Beron and his brothers, most likely so he wouldn't lose his position of heir. Eris needs to remain heir to change Autumn for the better when Beron finally dies. 

### ''[[A Court of Silver Flames]]''
Eris meets with Cassian several times throughout the novel to let the Night Court know of his father, Beron's plans and alliance with one of the evil queens Hybern made immortal using the Cauldron, named [[Briallyn|Briallyn,]] to start a new war. Eris first meets Cassian when he visits the human realm and inquires if [[Vassa]], [[Jurian]], and Lucien know anything about a missing patrol of his men who have vanished. Eris later reveals that he knows they have been taken by the old queen, but wanted to see if the humans knew anything more. 

Throughout the novel, Eris meets with Cassian in the Spring Court to keep each other updated with Beron's movements and what the Night Court has planned to stop him. Eris manages to get under Cassian's skin by constantly insulting him about his station and bastardry, which Cassian is more affected by than he cares to be. He also flirts with [[Nesta Archeron]], telling her that when she's done playing with Cassian she should see how real Fae play. After some time meeting, Eris is invited to the [[Hewn City]] where he learns of Feyre's pregnancy and is gifted one of Nesta's [[Made]] [[Nesta's Dagger|daggers]]. He is surprised Rhys has left this weapon hidden so long, but gratefully accepts it because of their alliance. 

During the celebration in Hewn City, Eris dances with Nesta. He is taken with her, and approaches Rhysand and offers him anything in exchange for her hand in marriage. He knows Rhys is using Nesta as a political pawn, but says he has his own reasons for wishing to wed her that he does not care to share. Eris leaves the celebration after Rhys tells him he will consider his offer and ask Nesta. 

Later in the novel, Eris is captured by Briallyn after approaching some of his missing soldiers. He then is influenced by [[the Crown]], and unknowingly sets a trap for Cassian and Azriel when they go to find him. Azriel manages to rescue him while Cassian is captured, and Az flies away with him to the safety of the Night Court. 

Eris, recovered from the control of the Crown, meets again with Cassian to explain that he has not revealed their allegiance to Beron. He reveals that he always mixes some truth when he lies under his father's torture, to make it that much more believable, showing a wounded side to him. Eris squirms multiple times, most likely from lashes on his back which still have not healed, alluding to the assumption that faebane must have been used. While discussing this, he tells Cassian not to pity him over his torture by the hands of his father and becomes defensive. He again maintains that Morrigan knows the truth of his actions and tells Cassian that he isn't the person he wants to explain himself to, hinting that there is more to what Mor is telling them.

## Appearance
He is described as having red hair, amber eyes, and pale skin. It is implied his hair is long because in ''[[A Court of Wings and Ruin]]'', when he met the Inner Circle and Keir in the Hewn city Feyre notes that it was left unbound and describes it as a "silken drape over his well-tailored cobalt jacket."

## Personality
Eris is a [[High Fae]] and, as the eldest of Beron's sons, he is presumed to be the heir to the Autumn Court.

Eris projects an air of arrogance and entitlement and can be antagonistic and violent at times. However, from the things he says and hints in the text, it seems there are deeper layers to him that reflect compassion, caring, and even true kindness at times. It is implied that his ruthlessness is a by-product of growing up under his father's cruelty, as well as having to constantly contend with his scheming younger brothers to avoid being betrayed and murdered by them in their quest to seize his position as heir. Like Rhysand, Eris appears to play-act to achieve his goals.

Although he has done many bad things in his life, Eris confessed in ''[[A Court of Wings and Ruin]]'' that he had no part in the execution of Jesminda, Lucien's lover, and that he had even alerted Tamlin to save his brother. Eris admitted that he had ended the engagement with Morrigan long ago because she did not want it. This shows his motives are sometimes benevolent, and that he is rather perceptive. It is a possibility that Eris pretends to be such a cruel, ruthless [[Faerie|Fae]] due to being born into the Autumn Court, and sired by Beron.

## Trivia
* Eris owns 12 smokehounds. These hounds are the best in Prythian. Canines blessed with magic of their own. Gray and sleek like smoke. They could race as fast as the wind. Sniff out any prey. They were so highly prized that the Autumn Court forbade them from being given or sold beyond its borders. And so expensive that only its nobility owned them. And they were bred rarely enough that even one was extremely difficult to come by.

This '''unnamed Fae''' was a [[High Fae]] who lived [[Under the Mountain]].

== History ==
=== '' [[A Court of Thorns and Roses]] '' ===
This Fae was forced to live Under the Mountain during [[Amarantha]]'s reign of terror as High Queen of [[Prythian]].

When [[Feyre Archeron]] showed up there to save [[Tamlin]] and his [[Spring Court|Court]], Amarantha told her that she had to pass three trials. The third trial consisted of murdering three Fae by driving an [[ash wood]] dagger into their hearts. This woman was the second of them that Feyre had to kill, which she did but not without the greatest regret. The woman gave her consent, knowing that it was the only way for the rest to be free from Amarantha.

== Trivia ==
* She was not part of the [[Spring Court]] as she did not have a mask at the time of her execution.
* In ''[[A Court of Thorns and Roses]]'' it was said that she had bronze eyes and in ''[[A Court of Mist and Fury]]'' it was said that she had blue eyes, which is a mistake.
* It was not a mistake when it was mentioned she had blue eyes in ''[[A Court of Mist and Fury]]''. This was a dream Feyre had, when she was revealed to be the girl once the hood was removed.

She was originally [[mortal]], but was [[Made|resurrected]] as [[High Fae]] by the seven High Lords after her untimely death [[Under the Mountain]]. Originally forced to leave the mortal lands after killing a fae to uphold the treaty, she trades her life in exchange, remaining in the [[Spring Court]] until she journeys [[Under the Mountain]] to break its curse, ultimately freeing all of [[Prythian]] from fifty years of [[Amarantha]]’s rule.  Upon returning to the [[Spring Court]], however, she eventually leaves to escape her relationship with [[Tamlin]], [[High Lord]] of the [[Spring Court]].

For some time, Feyre resides at the Court of Dreams in [[Velaris]]. She trains as an [[Illyrian]] warrior and hones her magic as part of the [[Night Court]]’s [[Inner Circle]], while working against [[Hybern]]. Although, during ''[[A Court of Mist and Fury]]'', and due to the events in [[Hybern]], she returns to the [[Spring Court]] as a spy.  Eventually, Feyre returns to the [[Night Court]] as High Lady, helping lead [[Prythian]] into war against [[Hybern]]. By the conclusion of the trilogy, Feyre remains in Velaris, reigning over the Night Court alongside [[Rhysand]], her [[mate]], husband, and [[High Lord]] of the [[Night Court]].

Feyre has power from each one of the seven [[Courts of Prythian|High Lords of Prythian]] ''(see "Powers and Abilities" below),'' and rises as High Lady. She is one of the few to successfully wield the [[Cauldron]] and the one to [[Made|Remake]] it during the final battle in ''[[A Court of Wings and Ruin|A Court of Wings and Ruin.]]''  

## History
### Early Life
Feyre is the youngest child of her family, with two older sisters [[Nesta]] and [[Elain]]. Feyre's family was wealthy; her [[Feyre's Father|father]] was called the "prince of merchants". Her [[Feyre's Mother|mother]] was a cold woman who cared only about her status and throwing parties. She was abrasive to Feyre calling her a “weird” and “sullen” child. Her mother neglected her, so she often spent time alone in her father’s study. 

Feyre's mother died of typhus when she was eight, but not before making her promise that she will take care of the family. When Feyre was nine, her father put all their money into a trading business across the sea, which resulted in losing all his ships. He was then hunted down and assaulted by [[creditors]]; Feyre was the only daughter to witness and attempt to stop his assault, as Nesta and Elain had fled into a bedroom. As a result of the attack, her father's knee was irreparably damaged. The family was soon forced to move out of their home and into a cottage in a nondescript [[Feyre's Village|village]]. 

Now living in poverty, Feyre became the provider for the family who left her with the sole burden. As a result, Feyre developed a no-nonsense, survalisist—almost cold mindset—to get through life. During these years, their father remained passive and withdrawn, offering little to no support. [[Nesta Archeron|Nesta]] frequently berated and belittled Feyre, spending much of her time seeking suitors. [[Elain Archeron|Elain]], while gentle and kind, largely occupied herself with gardening and spending time with Nesta. All three benefited from the money Feyre earned and relied entirely on what she provided.  

Feyre grew up illiterate and without the upper-class training her sisters benefited from. Instead, she learnt other skills. When she was eleven, she started watching hunters and learned to set snares and hunt. 

Feyre has always had an interest in painting; her late mother once thought about how her painting abilities might secure her a future husband. One time, Feyre was able to sell some of her hunts, and Elain bought her three paints—red, blue, and yellow. Feyre started painting on every surface she could find, tables, and a shared [[Feyre's Dresser|dresser]]. She painted fire for Nesta, flowers for Elain, and although she could not explain it, she painted the night sky, stars, and moon for herself.

At some point in her late teens, Feyre began a sexual relationship with an engaged village boy named [[Isaac Hale]] while her sisters searched for possible suitors.

### ''[[A Court of Thorns and Roses]]''
Feyre is first introduced in the snow-covered forests of her village as she hunts for food; it is the middle of a harsh winter and the [[Archeron Family|Archeron Family]] is close to starvation. She spots a doe in the forest, gets ready to shoot her bow, and then notices a golden eyed, large [[Andras|wolf]] on the opposite side of the forest clearing. Feyre struggles between deciding whether to run or shoot the large wolf, which she suspects could be a [[faerie]]. Out of hatred for the faeries, she shoots the unmoving, staring wolf with her only [[ash wood]] arrow, and since it does not die, she shoots another arrow that hits the middle of its eye, finally killing the creature. Feyre skins the wolf and drags a doe she killed to her [[Archeron's Cottage|cottage]]. The next day she goes to the market with her sisters to sell the wolf pelt and deer hide and met a [[mercenary]] who quickly bought them and paid her very well for them, more than expected.

That night, while having dinner with her family, a beast with a bear-like body, wolf head, and elk horns destroys her cottage's door and storms into it looking for the murderer of his friend, thus confirming that the wolf was a faerie. When Feyre confesses it was her, as punishment for having killed a faerie, Feyre is given two options, to die at that moment or to go live in Prythian, the land of the fairies that is deadly for a human being. Her father encourages her to do it and to never come back, saying that they never deserved her anyway. Feyre leaves with the beast and for the few minutes they move through the woods, she looks for a way to run away or kill it. She is then knocked out by the beast with magic and kept unconscious throughout the whole trek back to Prythian.

Feyre regains consciousness when she is already in the Spring Court and arrives at a nice, seemingly empty [[Tamlin's Manor|manor]]. She is taken into a dining room by the beast who, with a flash of light, transforms into a blond, young man with a mask, [[Tamlin]]. He offers her food, which she refuses, since she was raised to believe that all their food was enchanted or drugged. He sends her to have a bath in her room, which she is surprised by, since she thought she was his prisoner and would be living in a cell. This is when Feyre is introduced to [[Alis]]; Tamlin's maid. When she returns, Tamlin is still seated at the table, and she refuses to eat again. They are now joined by a faerie named [[Lucien]]— who initially mocks her for being human and looking starved. Tamlin attempts to compliment her, saying how she looked cleaned and well dressed, which she dismisses as pathetic flirting. Due to her continued distrust in the High Lord, he magically chains her to the seat and promises not to release her until she eats. Finally, Feyre obliges, and then is allowed back to her room.

Although initially the relationship with Tamlin is unwelcomed, he eventually gains Feyre's trust and interest. At first she tried to escape and feeling that she is failing the promise she made to her mother and leaving her family to die, Tamlin confesses that he glamoured her family to think she left to take care of a sick [[Aunt Ripleigh|aunt]] and her father's ships were found across the sea. Tamlin himself sent them gold and jewels to ensure their well-being after he took Feyre. With Tamlin's reassurance that her family is safe, she decides to stay, starts to pursue Tamlin and painting, while also trying to learn how to read.

During the night of the [[Calanmai]], Feyre is advised by Tamlin to stay in her room and not open her door no matter what. Her curiosity and the tempting sound of drums lure her to the festive in the hills.

While there, she meets three High Fae, who take an interest in her and herd her to the woods, where they attempt to take advantage of her. A stranger cuts in and passively tells the three High Fae to leave. The stranger, later revealed to be [[Rhysand]], offers to escort her elsewhere. She denies his offer and they end up parting ways. Feyre is then found by Lucien, who is furious she left the manor and promptly takes her back.

When Feyre hears the music from the hills suddenly stop, she assumes the festival is over and strolls out of her room to have a snack, only to be found by a Tamlin who is drunk with magic. He starts seducing her and bites her neck— to which she slaps him and claims that she is not his Calanmai seconds. Although she denies his attempt at seduction, their sexual tension seems to become more apparent after the confrontation.

One morning while Feyre and the two Fae males were having breakfast, the faerie who saved her during Calanmai enters the manor. Before he gets to the dining room, Lucien glamours Feyre to not be visible to the stranger. Tamlin reveals the stranger to be Rhysand and tries to kick him out. Rhysand reveals that he came to the Spring Court to pass a message from an unknown woman, later revealed to be [[Amarantha]]. Before Rhysand leaves, he notices the extra plate on the table and recognizes Feyre's smell. He uses his magic to reveal Feyre, and tells Tamlin that Amarantha would not like to find out about the presence of the human girl in his court at all. Rhysand uses his [[daemati]] abilities and inflicts pain onto Feyre. Tamlin and Lucien beg Rhysand not to tell Amarantha about Feyre. After asking her what her name is and she lies saying her name is [[Clare Beddor]], he leaves.

After this, Tamlin sends Feyre back to her human village as he says it is not safe for her to stay at Court. While Feyre is leaving the manor, Tamlin expresses that he loves her. She has the urge to say it back to him, but is unable to, and so she looks away from the High Lord. When she returns to the human lands, she finds that was her family was indeed cared for. Nesta confesses that she resisted Tamlin's glamour and is the only one who remembers everything that happened the night Feyre was taken to Prythian. During the next few weeks, Feyre's sadness and regret for not telling Tamlin she loves him increases.

Upon learning of the fire that killed the [[Beddor Family|Beddor family]], Feyre realizes they died because she gave the name Clare to Rhysand, so she understands that Amarantha has done something terrible in Prythian. She decides to go back to the Spring Court, which she then finds deserted and destroyed, except for Alis, a servant faerie who tells her the story of the curse that envelopes Prythian and accompanies her to the entrance of [[Under the Mountain]] so that she can rescue Tamlin from Amarantha. Once in the passages that lead to Under the Mountain she is caught by the [[Attor]], a creature in Amarantha's service, who brings her before her. Once in the High Queen of Prythian's throne room, Feyre confesses her love for Tamlin and Amarantha tells her that she can free him, along with all his court, if she survives three trials and solves a [[Amarantha's Riddle|riddle]]. Tamlin stays silent and unmoving throughout the whole interaction between the ladies.

During her first trial, Feyre hurts her arm, which is left unattended by Amarantha and the other faeries. One night, Rhysand manages to get into Feyre's cell and offers her healing in exchange for spending two weeks out of every month in the [[Night Court]]. Feyre initially disagrees, and Rhysand grabs the shard of bone that sticks out of her arm and twisting. After this torture and noticing that she will almost certainly die from the infection before Lucien can sneak away to help her, she realizes she has no other choice. She negotiates to lower the terms to only one week per month. Once they both agree, Rhysand grabs her injured arm and painfully heals her. A black tattoo with a cat's eye in the palm of her hand appears on her left arm as a permanent reminder of the deal she struck.

In between the trials, Rhysand protects Feyre from receiving extra torture from the errands assigned by the guards. However, he makes her wear transparent slips that barely cover her body that she finds humiliating. He forces her to drink a special wine that makes her completely lose control of her body and her memories. He treats her like a possession and forces her to do sensual dances or sit on his lap. He adds paint along her body to know if someone touched her. She spends her days sleeping off the faerie wine, dozing to escape the humiliation she endures. Rhysand’s treatment of Feyre during those nights were so horrific that Lucien couldn’t even begin to describe it to Feyre when she’d asked him in the next morning. All of this is part of a plan that he himself articulated, to make Tamlin jealous and when he was released, his wave of hatred would be so great that it could kill Amarantha. One night, Rhysand forcibly and harshly kisses Feyre against her will and without her consent because he was jealous of Tamlin.  

Rhysand saves Feyre once while she tries to complete the second trial. She threatens to break soon after and he speaks in his mind, to stay firm. And near the third trial, Rhysand saves her once again when she goes into a panic attack and doesn't feel anything. She herself says that she has sunk so deep inside herself that she will need something extraordinary to bring her back. He sends her a song through the ventilation hole in the ceiling of her cell. The music was something to be worshipped, Feyre said, to fill the void in her soul and take her to a place where there was no pain. She cried without realizing it, but she clung to the music and also to the visions she could see through it. A great palace in the sky, a hall of alabaster and opal, where everything that was beautiful and kind and fantastic lived in peace. Everything she wanted was there, the one she loved was there. 

After successfully completing the remaining two trials, Amarantha, enraged, starts killing Feyre, who eventually manages to solve Amarantha's riddle and break the curse with her last breath. Amarantha snaps Feyre's neck and she dies.

Feyre is later resurrected by the seven High Lords of Prythian, but she is turned in the process into a High Fae. Traumatized by all the things she had to do to free Tamlin, herself, and the whole of Prythian, she shares a goodbye with Rhysand and then returns to the Spring Court to live with Tamlin.

### ''[[A Court of Mist and Fury]]''
The book starts with a nightmare. The last person [[Amarantha]] makes her kill is herself and Feyre willingly plunges the ash dagger in her own heart.  Feyre has spent three months in the Spring Court after the events of Under the Mountain. She battles with the traumatic events she experienced that marked her deeply, having killed two innocent High Fae as part of her tests. She constantly has nightmares of being tortured by Amarantha, of killing, of her time Under the Mountain and vomits every night in the bathroom. Tamlin, dealing with his own trauma, ignores what is going on with both of them, pretending nothing happened. Feyre's physical and mental health consistently deterioriate and Tamlin rarely allows her out of his manor out of fear and misguided attempt at protecting her. Feyre spends her time planning her wedding along with [[Ianthe]], a [[High Priestess]], and Tamlin's childhood friend. Ianthe takes over the responsibility of wedding planner and controls every aspect of Feyre's life, including what she wears. Feyre, being so broken and tired, accepts everything she wants to do with her.

According to Ianthe, Feyre's responsibilities are to bear children to ensure Tamlin's bloodline survives, and plan parties. She has to behave according to his desires to send the "right message" to the remaining courts. 

She asks Lucien for help in convincing Tamlin to let her help and be productive, and while occasionally she can go out riding with sentries, Feyre wants to help and be useful or else she will drown in her trauma. Lucien struggles to convince Tamlin, often having to back down to his wishes. It is slowly revealed Feyre seems to have obtained magic from being resurructed. Lucien and Tamlin spend much of their time hunting down the remnants of Amarantha’s creatures still prowling their lands, leaving Feyre alone at the manor. Wanting to do something meaningful, she asks to visit the nearby fae village that is trying to rebuild, but Tamlin forbids it and refuses to let her go.

The day before, Lucien killed five naga and was injured but lied to Feyre, claiming he had fallen from his horse. Tamlin continues to justify restricting Feyre’s movements by insisting he can’t focus on his duties if he’s worried about her safety. He urges her to stay inside and paint, even though Feyre hasn’t been able to paint since returning from Under the Mountain.

Tamlin also insists she dress in bright gowns rather than the tunics and pants she prefers, saying her clothing sends a message across the land. Feyre keeps her dagger with her regardless, secured in the belt Lucien gifted her. After Feyre and Tamlin have sex, he apologizes for refusing to let her visit the village, insisting that he just needs her to be safe and “all right.” Feyre then asks what she will be called once they’re married, assuming she would become a High Lady. Tamlin corrects her, saying there is no such thing as a High Lady and that she will simply be the Lady of the Spring Court. High Lords only take wives or consorts, he tells her, and there has never been a High Lady in Prythian’s history.

The next day, Lucien sneaks Feyre out to the village. When she asks why he lied about his injury, he admits Tamlin ordered him not to tell her. He claims Tamlin is giving her “as much freedom as he can,” and that Feyre doesn’t understand how hard it is for Tamlin to let her off the grounds. She replies that she didn’t realize she’d become a prisoner. Lucien says Tamlin fears someone will use her to hurt him, but Feyre argues she can’t stay trapped in the manor forever. Lucien responds with a jab about “human women” wanting a rich faerie lord, and reminds her that as Tamlin’s future wife, there are traditions and expectations she must uphold. He also tells her Tamlin is calling the Tithe for the first time since the curse ended. Feyre will be expected to sit beside Tamlin as he collects payments and punishes anyone who fails to pay. Lucien excuses Tamlin’s behavior by saying he watched Feyre die and is terrified of losing her again. When they reach the village, no one will accept their help. He explains the Spring Court fae spent fifty years imprisoned underground in lightless camps—they are still recovering.

Guests begin arriving for the wedding. The night before, her weddingFeyre has another nightmare. Being trapped inside the manor makes her panic, and she can’t go into enclosed rooms without having a panic attack. On the day of her wedding, Feyre is not sure of what she is doing. She thinks after being married, things will be better between her and Tamlin and eventually, a mating bond will occur. [[Alis]] mentions that she looks as if she is going to a funeral instead of her wedding.

On the day of the wedding, Feyre gets dressed and quietly admits she hates the gown. As she walks down the aisle, she notices the red petals scattered along the path—too much like blood, too much like Under the Mountain—and panic spikes. She feels sick, claustrophobic, and starts begging in her mind for someone to help her, to get her out, to stop this. When Ianthe urges her forward to be joined to her “true love,” Feyre is moments away from refusing the marriage altogether—when Rhysand suddenly appears. Tamlin realizes what is happening and tries to grab her and pull her towards him. Suddenly [[Rhysand]] appears to take Feyre away to the Night Court, calling in the bargain she made with him Under the Mountain.

They both [[Winnowing|winnow]] to the [[Night Court]], where she finds a great house on a mountain, instead of the horrible place she expected. Feyre is surprised to see concern in Rhysand's eyes as he notes she has lost weight, but while he makes fun of her wedding dress and fiasco, she throws one of her shoes at his head. 

Feyre tells Rhys he knows nothing about her, and he asks if Tamlin does. He mentions Tamlin never asks why she vomits sick at night or can’t stand certain rooms or colors—things Rhys knows because she sends them down the bond. She asks how the bond works, and he says to imagine it as a bridge with a door to each of their minds. Her shields vanish when she’s distressed, and her thoughts hit him like she’s shouting. He only checks her mind when he needs to be sure a nightmare isn’t a real threat, and he offers to teach her how to guard against someone like him.

The next morning Rhysand tells Feyre her "torture" for the week, learning to read. He also offers her a job to stay with him and work for him, which she refuses automatically out of hate and anger towards him. He notes she is rather strong for a normal High Fae when she folds her fork at dinner and that she might have inherited the powers from other High Lords, while they resurrected her, and that she needs to train to be prepared for an upcoming war against the [[King of Hybern]] and his forces. Rhys says they had Feyre wrapped up like a present for Tamlin on their wedding day, as if she was his reward. Her only response is''"so?"'' which angers him. Feyre begs Rhys not to invade the Mortal Lands, and he tells her Hybern—not him—has been planning that war for a century. Amarantha was only a test, and Prythian will fall first. He fought for humans in the first war, and he’s warning her now because Tamlin has ties to Hybern. He also needs Feyre’s skills, reminding her she caught the Suriel when he couldn’t. Rhys believes Feyre’s power rivals a High Lord’s and that she could be a High Lady. With the powers of all seven High Lords, she could change the outcome of the coming war. Feyre says Tamlin won’t let her train, but Rhys insists she isn’t his subject. She can either be a pawn and a trophy, or master her abilities and fight. Rhys leaves her alone for the rest of the week, sending only reading exercises and reminders to practice her mental shields.

When Feyre is returned to the Spring Court, she tries to be intimate with Tamlin but instead Tamlin wants to press her for information about the Night Court. She feels like she is being interrogated but she tells them everything, including the fact Rhys thinks she might have the powers of the High Lords. Lucien says that if that is true then some High Lords may wish to kill her. Tamlin doesn't want her to train and learn how to use her powers. He says he can protect her from anything and that there will be no training.

During the Tithe, a water-wraith arrives with nothing to offer because her lake has no fish. Feyre argues they don’t need the goods, they have more than enough, but he insists it’s tradition. Feyre gives the wraith her jewelry to sell so she can pay and buy food. The wraith promises her people won’t forget. That night, Tamlin is furious she “made him look weak.” Lucien defends her, but Tamlin snaps at him too. Feyre, frustrated, accidentally slips into Lucien’s mind and then notices burn marks on the table where her hands touched—another sign that something is happening to her power.  Dutifully ignoring it, not sure what she can do and afraid to consider it. 

When Rhysand takes her to the Night Court for a second time, she spends most of her time alone and reading books, after learning to read and write under Rhysand's teachings and teasing, the characters and stories helping distract her from her trauma. He also teaches her to block her mind from anyone that may attempt to control her. When she is about to return to the Spring Court, concerned, he asks her what he can do to help her, since she is looking physically and mentally worse every day. She again refuses his help but expresses that she is not so excited about going back to the Spring Court.

Tamlin apologizes by giving Feyre a traveling paint kit—despite her not having painted in months. She tells him she can’t live under his restrictions forever, especially now that she’s fae. He insists danger still exists, and she tells him maybe he should marry someone willing to endure it. When she admits she feels like she’s drowning, Tamlin snaps and his power explodes, shattering windows, furniture, and the gift. Feyre shields herself just in time. Tamlin apologizes again and promises to try, but they avoid addressing anything and fall back into sex instead.

Later, Feyre overhears Tamlin arguing with Rhys. Rhys notices how thin she’s gotten and asks if she’s even being fed. Feyre realizes she feels safer in the Night Court than in the Spring Court. Tamlin offers Rhys anything if he’ll end their bargain; Rhys refuses and takes Feyre with him. Back at the Night Court, Feyre changes and notices her clothes hang off her—she’s lost far more weight than she realized. Rhys makes her eat with him, saying he’ll always make time for her. He tells her he felt a spike of fear through the bond the day Tamlin lost control. She refuses to talk about it. Rhys asks if she’s thought about his offer, and Feyre refuses, insisting he only wants her to spite Tamlin. She says she won’t jeopardize her relationship and that Tamlin is trying. Rhys then asks what ''he'' can do to help her, whether anyone else even asks how she’s doing—whether Tamlin truly cares. Feyre insists he does, that he’s giving her “space,” even as she feels Rhys’s anger rising through the bond.

Feyre notices claws forming on her hand—Tamlin’s power—and Lucien pulls her aside to explain her abilities will surface whether she trains or not. He promises to talk to Tamlin. Feyre later overhears Lucien, Tamlin, and Ianthe discussing how some High Lords might try to kill her for having their power, while others might try to claim her for children who could inherit it. They even consider killing Rhys to break the bargain. Lucien begs Tamlin to let her train so she can defend herself, but Tamlin refuses, claiming training would expose her abilities. He has another outburst, and that night Feyre locks her door and doesn’t eat. The next day, she asks to accompany them on patrol. Tamlin refuses again. She begs, reminding him he promised to do better, but he says her “untrained abilities” make her a liability. When she insists she’s going regardless, he seals the entire manor with magic to trap her inside. Lucien confirms she can’t leave. Feyre panics, tries to escape, tries even to winnow—nothing works. Her power erupts in a cocoon of darkness, fire, and ice, melting her engagement ring. Someone lifts her, and for a moment she thinks it’s Amarantha—until she realizes it’s Mor. Mor tells her Rhys shattered the shield with half a thought and that she’s free. She brings Feyre to him, assuring Rhys she followed procedure “to the letter.”

When Feyre awakes the next day she finds Rhysand in her room, tired and desperate, after spending the night in a chair watching over her. He tells her she is free to stay in the [[House of Wind]] until she desires to leave. Feyre asks him to take her with him and he winnows them to [[Velaris]]. After meeting Rhysand's [[Inner Circle]], and seeing Velaris Feyre decides to work with him. 

Feyre feels anger, realising that he protected Velaris while other places suffered. Rhys explains the city has been hidden for millennia by wards and spells; no other court, not even Amarantha, knew it existed. Opening it as a refuge would have exposed it and doomed everyone who lived there. He shows her the city’s famous market palaces and the Rainbow—its artists’ quarter filled with theaters, galleries, and shops. She asks who will be at dinner, and he lists Mor, Cassian, Azriel, and Amren. Amren is his Second, Mor his Third. He warns Feyre that Amren only looks High Fae—she’s far older and extremely dangerous.

Feyre feels herself panic, after everything she's been through realizing it would just be easier to die, Rhys tells her to not ever think that. When he touches her, she accidentally slips into his mind and sees herself as he sees her—thin, exhausted, and miserable. Rhys says only daemati can do that, meaning she likely inherited the ability from him.

Feyre gets angry again, saying Velaris will stay safe while others die if Hybern attacks. Rhys doesn’t argue but says he’ll have to fly her to the House of Wind, since he can’t winnow into it. She eventually agrees. He tells her he used to sneak out of the house to fly with his mother and admits he thought he’d never see this place again during his 50 years Under the Mountain. Feyre reflects that she feels foolish—she let herself be kept ignorant in the Spring Court and might’ve spent eternity as a sheltered pet. She realizes she fell for Tamlin because she was lonely and desperate for safety, and that version of love no longer fits the person she’s becoming.

Cassian and Azriel arrive. Amren tells Feyre they are alike—creatures born into the wrong bodies. Azriel explains siphons help focus Illyrian power, which is naturally destructive. Rhys adds that Cassian and Azriel aren’t lords; Mor is the only titled one in the Inner Circle. None of them were Under the Mountain because Amarantha didn’t know they existed, and Feyre sees the guilt they all carry over what Rhys endured alone. They explain how they met: Cassian and Azriel were both unwanted bastards sent to brutal Illyrian camps. Rhys also trained there, unable to use magic because he was too strong. He and Cassian hated each other until Azriel arrived, but Rhys eventually took them both in. His father later separated them out of fear their combined power would rival his own. During the war, they were split up, and Rhys was later captured—he ends the story there. Once Rhys became High Lord, he chose his Inner Circle—Cassian, Azriel, Mor, and Amren—and told his father’s poisonous court to leave. Those who hated him now rule themselves in the Hewn City, the Court of Nightmares, while Velaris stands as the Court of Dreams. Mor says she was born in Nightmares but always a dreamer. They ask Feyre’s story, she shares it, and Cassian offers to teach her to fight—making Feyre realize how false Tamlin’s excuses were. She tells Rhys she’ll help him. Rhys explains Hybern plans to start a war and resurrect Jurian, likely using the stolen eye and finger. To understand how, he needs to speak to the Bone Carver, who might only talk to Feyre because she’s Made with a human soul. Feyre agrees.

Feyre asks Rhys about feeling him at dinner. He says the bond is a living channel shaped by what she needed—someone who wouldn’t let her be alone. She realizes the Court of Nightmares persona was a mask. Rhys admits the cost of keeping Velaris safe Under the Mountain—playing Amarantha’s whore, mentally controlling every captured Night Court citizen daily so they’d forget the city and those he loved. He only had enough power to hide one place, so he chose Velaris. Feyre thinks the rest of Prythian should know; Rhys says only the safety of his people matters.

That night, she has another nightmare of Amarantha and wakes with flame ablaze in her bedroom-claws tearing that have torn through her bedding and set fire to her room. Rhys comforts her, holds her hair as she’s sick, and admits he has nightmares too. 

The next day Feyre and Rhysand visit the Prison, where at first Feyre is unable to enter due to her trauma from Under the Mountain. Amren gives her an amulet, vehemently stating that she has to give it back, but that Amulet got her out of the prison and it would help Feyre too. 

Rhys explains the Prison is ancient and self-governing, but his blood can open its gates. No one who enters can ever leave. Amren was imprisoned here long before the Courts existed—likely for millennia. Feyre asks where Amren came from, and Rhys says some believe ancient creatures slipped into their world through rips when the realms were first formed and became trapped when the rifts closed. As for the Bone Carver, Rhys says he appears differently to everyone. When they enter his cell, he looks like a dark-haired, blue-eyed boy to Feyre. Rhys offers him the bone Feyre used to kill the Middengard Wyrm. Rhys warned Feyre: never lie, and always trade a question for a question. The Carver asks where she went when she died. Feyre explains she heard her spine snap, then saw only darkness—until she sensed a thread that pulled her into Rhys’s mind. A scrap of her soul survived because of their bargain, and when she was Made, she followed that thread back to her body.

Feyre then asks if a person with only a bone left could be resurrected if their soul still existed. The Carver says yes—but only through the Cauldron, the source of all magic. It was hidden long ago, and its three feet were cut off and scattered to weaken it. If those feet have been taken, someone has likely found the Cauldron itself. Pressed for who has it, the Carver finally admits: the King of Hybern. Feyre gives him another truth to earn more answers—she reveals she planned to kill herself Under the Mountain if the last faerie hadn’t been Tamlin. She lived only because Amarantha killed her before she could do it. The Carver says Hybern might use the Cauldron to break the wall. Its power ''can'' be stopped: when the Cauldron was first made, the last of its ore was forged into the [[Book of Breathings]], which holds the spell to negate or control its power. The Book was split in two—one half with the Summer Court, warded by their High Lord’s blood, and the other with the mortal queens. Only someone Made can use it, which means Feyre—and Jurian, if resurrected—could wield it.

As they leave, the Carver says he’ll carve Feyre’s death into the bone and reveals he appeared to Rhys as Jurian the last time he saw him.

Back in Velaris, Rhys tells the Inner Circle what the Bone Carver revealed. Azriel offers to ask his contacts at the Summer Court about their half of the Book, but Rhys refuses—he trusts no one else with that information. He thinks they may need to infiltrate Hybern for the Cauldron and wants to test whether Feyre can sense the Book using the fragment of Summer Court power she inherited. To do that, he wants her to retrieve an object of his from the Weaver’s trove. The others think he’s insane, but Rhys lets Feyre choose, and she agrees.

Rhys also asks Feyre to become the Night Court’s emissary to the human world. They’ll need the mortal queens’ half of the Book, and she’ll need a safe base—her sisters’ estate. Feyre realizes he taught her to read because he already suspected they would need the Book. He arms her with knives and warns that the [[Weaver]] is blind but deadly. Feyre must touch only the object that belongs to him.

Rhys takes Feyre to neutral territory. He irritates her on purpose to distract her, but she eventually enters the [[Weaver's Cottage|Weaver’s Cottage]] alone. Inside, she finds the [[Weaver's Song|Weaver singing]], ring—twisted gold and silver with a blue stone—but the Weaver senses her. Feyre bolts, burns the Weaver’s threads, crawls up a sticky chimney, and escapes onto the hair-covered roof before Rhys winnows her out. Covered in fat and hair, she vomits. Rhys says she survived because she saved herself, not because he intervened. Feyre asks Cassian to train her. The ring belonged to Rhys’s mother; she left it with the Weaver for safekeeping. Rhys tells Feyre she’ll train her other powers with him. He also reveals Ianthe once tried to seduce him and warns Feyre never to enter a mind without leaving herself an exit.

Mor explains that in the Court of Nightmares, powerful females are treated as property and sold off; her own story is terrible, but she’ll tell Feyre another time. Feyre, Rhys, Cassian, and Azriel then head to the mortal world—to her family’s estate.

Feyre rings the doorbell while Rhys, Cassian, and Azriel hide behind a glamour. She reveals her ears to her sisters and tells them she died and was [[Made]]. She explains everything—Amarantha, Hybern, the Cauldron, the Book, and the need for the mortal queens to meet here. [[Nesta Archeron|Nesta]] refuses, saying fae in their home would ruin their standing and jeopardize Elain’s engagement. [[Elain Archeron|Elain]] is set to marry a lord’s son in five months, and his family hunts fae. Nesta insists no fae will enter their house—including Feyre. Elain pushes back. She says there will be no wedding if they don’t help, and that they can dismiss their servants whenever the fae need the estate. Feyre asks about Elain’s fiancé. Nesta says Elain likes him well enough, though his [[Nolan's Estate|father’s estate]] is surrounded by a wall so tall it looks like a prison. His name is [[Graysen]]. Nesta then points out Feyre nearly died saving Tamlin, and now they’re not together. Feyre answers that one lord built walls to keep fae out—while the other tried to keep her locked inside.

Over dinner, Nesta is cold, asking if their food was not good enough for Feyre after seeing her make faces at it. Cassian snaps, reminding her Feyre hunted to keep them alive while Nesta did nothing, and that Feyre died for his people and would do it again to protect Nesta. He tells her not to sneer at Feyre for becoming fae when it wasn’t a choice. Elain tries to soften things, saying they grew up fearing fae and that [[Clare Beddor|Clare’s]] recent murder shook them. They decide to send a letter to the mortal queens in the morning. Feyre teases Rhys about his friends’ own drama, pointing out Azriel’s feelings for Mor. Rhys says dinner with her sisters reminded him how young she is—and how little they did to protect her. He also tells her she earns a salary for working for him and it’s already being saved for her in Velaris. He asks when her birthday is; she says [[Winter Solstice]].

The next day Rhys takes her to train. He wants her to practice fire, water, and air on a single candle, but she can’t focus with him watching and sends him away. She’s writing magic notes back and forth to him when a hand clamps over her mouth—the Attor. Rhys appears and pins the Attor to a tree. The creature says the King of Hybern sent it to capture Feyre and that Hybern’s endless army is coming, with allies everywhere. Azriel arrives and drags the Attor off to be tortured and made into a warning for the King.

Feyre realizes Rhys used her as bait—he knew someone was hunting her. They argue, and he tells her she keeps forgetting she has power. She attacks him, but he winnows away until she manages to winnow to him and tackles him into the snow. She tells him never to use her as bait again. He apologizes and suggests going home. She snaps that Velaris is ''not'' her home.

Rhys shows her what they got from interrogating the Attor. The Attor didn’t know how Hybern found her, only that the King sent the order. It taunts Rhys that he “stole” Feyre from Tamlin and says Tamlin has shut his borders. Rhys orders Azriel to break its legs, shred its wings, and dump it off Hybern’s coast. Feyre asks about the Spring Court, and Rhys warns her how far Tamlin will go to protect what he believes is his. Feyre writes Tamlin a letter saying she left by choice, is safe, and isn’t coming back. 

At dinner, Feyre asks Amren when the Night Court’s Tithe is. Amren says they don’t have one—city residents simply pay taxes. Feyre tries to return the amulet Amren lent her for the Prison, but Amren tells her to keep it; it has no magic, she only gave it so Feyre would ''believe'' she’d make it out alive.

They all have dinner in Velaris, and Feyre notices the cook brings Amren blood—her actual food. On the walk home, Feyre asks Rhys if the jewelry he buys Amren means they’re together. He says no—he once invited her to bed and she laughed. He explains he’s had lovers but never someone he wanted to marry, and anyone tied to him would become a target. Rhys tells her the Night Court was once ruled entirely from the Hewn City, a true Court of Nightmares, until an ancient High Lord overthrew it and created Velaris. Darkness eventually returned, so the court split. Rhys keeps the Court of Nightmares alive because its existence helps protect Velaris. As they walk, Feyre hears music she recognizes—the same music that reached her cell Under the Mountain. She realizes Rhys sent it to her to keep her from breaking, though he says he didn’t send the visions she saw with it. The next day they trade flirtatious notes.

Feyre trains with Cassian while Rhys and Azriel spar. She notices their Illyrian tattoos, earned during initiation. Cassian asks when she’ll talk about her letter to Tamlin; she fires back that he can talk when he admits what he feels for Mor. Feyre realizes she hasn’t thought about Tamlin much—aside from imagining him destroying the manor in rage. As she trains, anger floods her: how Tamlin sat beside Amarantha while she suffered, how he tried to sleep with her instead of saving her. She’s hitting Cassian’s pads until flames burn through them. 

She breaks down over the fae she killed, saying it should’ve been her. Rhys comforts her, saying he feels the same but learned to live with it. He admits he has two kinds of nightmares: being Amarantha’s whore again, or watching Feyre die.

The [[Summer Court]] finally agrees to receive them, so Feyre, Rhys, and Amren go. Feyre finds her and Rhys’s flirting an easy distraction. They arrive in [[Adriata]], the Summer Court’s coastal city. High Lord [[Tarquin]] greets them, along with his sister [[Cresseida]] and brother [[Varian]]. Rhys warns them of Hybern’s coming war. Tarquin worries [[Tamlin]] might start a war of his own to reclaim Feyre. Cresseida points out Tamlin could legally demand her return as his bride. Rhys warns that anyone sending word to Tamlin will die for it.

Rhys asks Feyre to steal the Summer Court’s half of the Book undetected. She jokes about seducing Tarquin to get it; Rhys says she’s free to do whatever she wants. Her water magic is easier to access around Tarquin, and she asks if Tamlin would really go to war for her. Rhys says he doesn’t know—only that Tamlin saw her value and wanted to keep her. Feyre admits Tamlin loved her, but “love can be a poison.”

Tarquin shows her his treasures and talks about wanting more equality in Prythian. Feyre tells him he’s easy to like. She sees Rhys flirting with Cresseida and is shocked to feel jealous—her first real emotion in a long time. Rhys disappears with Cresseida that night.

Tarquin brings Feyre to a jewel hall and gifts her a necklace of black diamonds to honor what she did Under the Mountain. He tells her he could be an ally to the North and admits Rhys saved a Summer Court rebel long ago by lying to Amarantha. Tarquin is young and unsure, but promises not to tell the Spring Court she’s visiting. Feyre tours their troves but can’t find the Book. Back in her room, Rhys is waiting on her bed. She urges him to just ask Tarquin for the Book. He denies sleeping with Cresseida and admits he’s jealous—he heard Feyre tell Tarquin he’d be easy to love, and that’s what bothers him.

Two days pass with no sign of the Book. Feyre notices a building revealed only when the tide goes out and suspects the Book is hidden there. At dinner, she mentions it and immediately sees from Tarquin and Cresseida’s reactions that she’s right. She slips into Tarquin’s mind to calm his suspicion. That night, Rhys and Amren meet her—Rhys knows exactly what she did with Tarquin’s mind and is impressed. They decide to go for the Book. Amren and Feyre go to the submerged [[Summer Court Temple|temple]] while Rhys keeps watch. They dig through the mud until they reach a lead door. Feyre fools the lock that she is Tarquin, then she and Amren descend into a hidden chamber. She finds a lead box on a dais. When she lies to the box the way she lied to the door, the Book calls her a liar and traps them inside. Amren blasts the door open, but water floods in. They nearly drown before the [[Water-wraith|water-wraiths]] tear the door away and rescue them—repaying the debt Feyre earned by helping their sister in the Spring Court. Rhys winnows them home as Summer Court alarms go off. The Book whispers to Feyre, calling her a liar, telling her “like calls to like.” It’s made of metal plates bound with gold, silver, and bronze rings, written in an ancient script—[[Leshon Hakodesh]]. Only Amren can translate it. Rhys admits he hoped it might also contain the spell that could free Amren of her binding.

They wonder why Hybern wants Jurian revived. Rhys guesses Jurian might want Miryam back—and vengeance on Drakon. The next day, a box of three huge [[Blood Ruby|blood rubies]] arrives: blood rubies. The Summer Court’s formal declaration that Feyre, Rhys, and Amren are marked for death. Rhys drinks, clearly upset—he had hoped for an alliance with Tarquin. Feyre flirts to cheer him up; they banter until Azriel interrupts. Later, Rhys sends her a vivid, teasing vision through the bond, and she’s so startled she walks into a post. That night, the house shakes with Rhys’s nightmares. Feyre finds him thrashing in darkness, tries to wake him, and he accidentally pins her by the throat before coming to. She notices the tattoos on his knees—a mountain with three stars above it, the Night Court's insignia. She kisses his cheek before leaving and imagines how she would paint him.

Feyre sends Rhys a note asking about the tattoos on his knees. He replies: ''“That I will bow before no one and nothing but my crown.”'' She visits Amren, bringing blood. [[Varian]] has sent Amren a [[Necklace of Diamonds and Rubies|ruby-and-diamond necklace]]—Amren says he can’t decide whether he wants her or hates her. Feyre keeps training: Illyrian blades with Cassian, magic with Rhys, though she still can’t winnow again. Feyre and Rhys continue exchanging notes. She tells him she can’t paint anymore—that part of her feels gone. When she asks if he always wanted to be High Lord, he says yes and no; he never wanted to rule the way his father did. They finally hear from the [[Mortal Queens|mortal queens]]. The meeting will be at Feyre’s family estate. The queens demand no weapons and ask for the exact layout of the house—and Feyre realizes why when they winnow directly inside it. Five queens arrive; Feyre notes they’re all different in age and temperament. Rhys asks about the missing sixth queen; they claim she’s ill. They already know of Mor’s reputation from the old war. The queens refuse to help. They say the human territory here is tiny compared to the rest of the world and not worth defending, and that the fae should fix the threat they created. They insist their half of the Book will never leave their palace. Feyre begs, and they condescend to her. Rhys tells them not to. Mor steps in, revealing her gift is truth. She speaks of fighting beside [[Miryam]] and freeing slaves in the Black Lands. She tells the queens Miryam and [[Drakon]] built a peaceful settlement on a hidden island and have lived there for 500 years—proof humans and fae ''can'' coexist.

The queens demand proof that Rhys is truly a High Lord of peace and not the monster they’ve heard about. Rhys realizes the only way to prove it is to show them Velaris. He tells them he’ll summon them again with their proof. They leave.

Feyre asks Rhys who Miryam and Drakon are and whether they could serve as proof instead of exposing Velaris. Rhys explains their history: centuries ago, the Black Land enslaved humans under a [[Queen of the Black Land|queen]] crueler than Amarantha. Miryam—half-fae, born of rape—was given as a “gift” to Prince Drakon, who let her escape. She later fell in love with Jurian, then met Drakon again and chose him. When Jurian was killed, Amarantha told him Miryam had betrayed him. Most think Miryam and Drakon died freeing their people, but they actually fled with them and built a hidden settlement. Rhys refuses to drag them into another war. To convince the mortal queens, Rhys decides to use the [[Veritas]]—an orb owned by Mor’s family that shows pure truth. They’ll steal it from the [[Hewn City|Court of Nightmares]]. Rhys hates bringing Feyre there; he doesn’t want her seeing the persona he must become in that place, afraid it will trigger memories of [[Under the Mountain]]. Feyre asks about Mor’s past. Rhys explains her family tried to sell her to Eris of the Autumn Court. To ruin the deal, she slept with Cassian. Her family brutalized her and dumped her at the Autumn border with a note claiming she was [[Eris Vanserra|Eris’s]] problem. Eris left her for dead; Azriel found her days later.

Feyre will play a role in the Court of Nightmares. To distract Rhys from worrying, she jokes about wing size and anatomy. When she touches his wings mid-flight, ash arrows shoot at them. They track the source with Azriel but find no one—this is the second time someone has known their location.

Feyre dresses in a revealing gown reminiscent of the UTM parties and wears a crown. Rhys removes the damper on his power and sits on his throne. Feyre is to act as his distraction—his “whore.” Rhys summons [[Keir]], Mor’s father. As Feyre sits on Rhys’s lap, Keir sneers that Tamlin’s pet now has a new master. When Keir calls Feyre a whore, Rhys snaps. He forces Keir to his knees, breaks both his arms when he refuses to apologize, and forbids him from seeing a healer. Only when Azriel signals that he has stolen the Veritas do they leave.

Rhys winnows Feyre to a quiet mountain lake and apologizes. He says he’ll never lock her up, but he lost control when Keir called her a whore, the same insult he endured for fifty years. Feyre tells him he needs to be better prepared, but he swears he’ll kill anyone who harms her. She cries and says she wants him to keep including her, not protecting her by shutting her out. Rhys snaps that she needs to stop comparing him to Tamlin; he will never treat her the way Tamlin did. Rhys asks what she wants. She can’t answer, and when she pushes back—telling him he hides behind masks even from his friends, because he's worried about the mess behind it—she realizes she’s hurt him. He takes her home. She hopes he’ll come to her that night so she can tell him she wants him, truly, but he doesn’t. He disappears from breakfast and lunch, and her notes won’t reach him. Rhys is hunting whoever shot the ash arrows—they’ve discovered someone tracked his magic. They plan to leave for an Illyrian war-camp in two days.

The next night is Starfall. Amren tells Feyre Rhys returned from UTM a ghost, and Feyre brought him back to life. Feyre keeps trying to send him notes, finally calling him a coward—only that one reaches him. He still doesn’t respond. Dressed for Starfall, Feyre is flown to the House of Wind by Cassian. Mor explains she only slept with Cassian once at seventeen, and things were messy between them, Azriel, and Rhys afterward.

Rhys finally appears and says he wasn’t ignoring her—he just needed time. When Feyre looks up, thousands of glowing spirits streak across the sky. He takes her onto a private balcony. She apologizes for what she said. Rhys explains Starfall: the lights aren’t stars but migrating spirits. Amarantha used to force him to service her on this night because she knew what it meant to him. Feyre reaches for him, but a spirit splashes across her face, coating her in green light. Another hits Rhys, and they both start laughing—really laughing—for the first time. She asks for the two thoughts he owes her from when she first came to the Night Court. He says he avoided her because he feared she’d reject him, and that he wishes he could take back their kiss from Under the Mountain. She asks if he wants to go dance with the others. He says yes and kisses her cheek.

Feyre and Rhys dance at Starfall, and he takes her home with a kiss to her brow. She feels guilty for wanting him when she left Tamlin only two months ago. Rhys, Mor, Cassian, and Feyre travel to the Illyrian war-camp. Feyre meets Devlon, the camp’s leader. Rhys orders him to train the females before chores and to prepare his mother’s old house for them. When Devlon asks what Feyre is, Rhys warns that no one is to touch her unless they want to die. During training, Feyre learns that wing clipping was outlawed, but some camps still do it. Cassian is trying to build a fighting unit of Illyrian females, but many quit because the males torment them or because camps decree trained females “unfit for marriage.” Rhys can’t override all of their customs. Rhys explains the Blood Rite—Illyrians dropped into the mountains unarmed with no magic or supplies. He, Cassian, and Azriel survived and fought their way to each other when they were young.

Feyre asks about Tamlin. Rhys agrees to tell her if she shows him something with her powers, so she shapes a butterfly from water. Rhys explains he met Tamlin at court; Tamlin seemed decent, unlike his brutal brothers. Rhys even taught him Illyrian techniques. Their families hated the friendship. When Rhys’s [[Rhysand's Mother|mother]] and [[Rhysand's Sister|sister]] traveled to visit him, Tamlin told his father where they’d be. Tamlin’s father and brothers went instead—murdered them, cut off their wings, and sent their heads downriver. Feyre realizes she saw those wings in Tamlin’s study. Rhys continues after Feyre freezes her water-butterflies into ice. He and [[Rhysand's Father|his father]] went to the Spring Court for revenge. Rhys killed  [[Tamlin's Brothers|Tamlin's brothers]]; his father killed Tamlin’s parents—including [[Tamlin's Mother|his mother]], despite promising not to. Tamlin then killed Rhys’s father. Both boys felt their power shift as they became [[High Lord|High Lords]]. Feyre finally understands Rhys’s hatred and why Tamlin never told her the truth. Fire blasts from her in anger. She now commands fire, ice, water, and darkness. She asks why Rhys never told her sooner. He says he didn’t want her to think he was trying to turn her against Tamlin. Feyre realizes she wants to paint again—starting with Rhys, who jokes that nude would be best.

Feyre and Rhys head deeper into the forest to train where no one can see them, planning to camp overnight. Feyre wonders if keeping things purely physical with him might feel like less of a betrayal. While she trains alone, four Spring Court sentinels and [[Lucien Vanserra|Lucien]] suddenly surround her. Lucien says they’ve been hunting for her for two months and that someone tipped them off. He tells her Tamlin hasn’t been himself and insists on taking her “home.” Feyre reminds him that home stopped being home the moment Tamlin locked her inside it. Lucien says it was a mistake and begs her to come back. When he tries to grab her, Feyre finally manages to winnow—and Rhys appears beside her. Lucien refuses to back down. Feyre tells him he chose to obey Tamlin even after seeing what it did to her. She begged him for help, and he did nothing. Lucien claims they needed to appear “unified.” Feyre shifts Illyrian wings onto herself and tells him the human girl he knew died Under the Mountain. She won’t spend eternity as anyone’s pet. If Tamlin sends more soldiers into Night Court lands, she’ll hunt them down herself.

They stay at a village inn—only one room left. Both soaked, they change clothes; Feyre asks how Rhys dresses with wings, and he says magic usually handles it. His magic needs constant release or it can turn on him. She asks what he would have done if Lucien dragged her away. He says he’d have torn the world apart to get her back. They finally admit they want each other. When he asks what she wants, she says she wants to have fun—leading to kissing and Rhys giving her an orgasm.

Feyre asks why he made their bargain Under the Mountain. Rhys says it was partly to spite Amarantha, partly to spite Tamlin, and mostly to keep Feyre alive in a way that didn’t look merciful. The next day, while flying, Rhys is hit with poisoned ash arrows and plummets, throwing Feyre clear. She can’t feel him through the bond and tracks him down through the woods until she finds him chained in a cave, seven ash arrows through his wings while two Hybern soldiers whip him. Feyre snaps and kills them all, moving with lethal precision. She cuts through the soldiers one by one—quick, efficient, and terrifyingly skilled.R hys can’t winnow because the ancient chains suppress his powers, so she winnows them both to another cave and begins pulling the arrows out. To distract him from the pain, she tells him about the drawers she once painted for her sisters: flowers for Elain, flames for Nesta, and a night sky for herself. 

Rhys isn’t healing because the ash arrows were laced with Bloodbane. Feyre hunts down the [[Suriel]] again and learns the cure: her own blood, strengthened by the [[Thesan|High Lord of Dawn’s]] healing power. A few drops will save Rhys. The Suriel adds that chewing a pink-flowered weed by the river will speed her ''[[Mate|mate’s]]'' recovery. Feyre freezes—Rhys is her mate, and he never told her. She returns furious. She gets Rhys to chew the plant, gives him her blood, and then demands answers. Rhys admits he suspected for a while but knew for certain when she died Under the Mountain—he felt the bond snap into place the moment he said goodbye. Amren and Mor know; Cassian and Az suspect. He says he wanted her to figure it out herself, and that when she left Tamlin she was already drowning in too much to handle. And recently, she told him she only wanted “fun,” not a mating bond. Feyre says he promised no secrets. She doesn’t care about his excuses; he took away her choice. She makes him winnow her back to camp.

She immediately finds Mor and asks her to take her somewhere else. Mor winnows her to a [[The Cabin|mountain cabin]] owned by their family. Feyre tells her she knows about the mating bond and begs her not to tell Rhys where she is. Mor agrees and says she’ll return in three days to check on her. Feyre realizes she ran away from Rhys—she didn’t let him explain, even after promising she wouldn’t do that. Inside the cabin she discovers paint and spends the entire day painting. When Mor returns, every surface is covered in color: small details of Mor, Cassian, Azriel, Rhys, and Amren woven into the walls.

Feyre asks Mor why Rhys still brings her to the Court of Nightmares after what they did to her. Mor says Rhys gave her permission to kill every one of them and she visits because she likes reminding them of it. Feyre asks about Azriel; Mor says he’ll never make a move because he still sees himself as a bastard unworthy of anyone. Feyre realizes she likes the idea of a future with Rhys. She stays five more days, painting every room in the cabin.

Rhys finally appears. Feyre offers to heat him soup, and he quietly tells her that when a female offers her mate food for the first time, it’s significant—it means she accepts the bond. Feyre asks Rhys to tell her everything. He explains that during the war, Amarantha’s soldiers captured his legion. They tortured and slaughtered his warriors for weeks, pinned his wings with ash, and chained him with Hybern-forged nullifying chains. He refused to break. He planned to kill Amarantha, but the day before, he was forced to watch her defeat and torture Jurian. Rhys’s father eventually rescued him but left the ash spikes in as punishment for being captured. Amarantha went unpunished—and when she returned centuries later, she didn’t even remember him. He went to her party Under the Mountain intending to kill her, but after drinking her wine, he lost most of his power—using what remained to shield Velaris.

Rhys then tells her about the dreams that started three years ago: dreams through ''her'' eyes. Human hands painting a table. A nightmare of her running from the Bogge. When he saw unlit Calanmai bonfires in one dream, he realized she was in the Spring Court and convinced Amarantha to let him go. He saved her from the faeries in the woods that night and realized she didn’t know who he was. He admits he later gave those fae to Amarantha, hoping it would lull her. He didn’t know Feyre was with Tamlin until Summer Solstice, when he found her hiding behind Lucien. He realized she loved Tamlin. He tried to scare Tamlin into sending her away. He asked her name because he needed to know it—and assumed she lied. That’s why he told Amarantha her name. He had no idea Clare was real until Amarantha dragged her in. He dulled Clare’s pain as she died and finally killed her to end it.

When Feyre arrived Under the Mountain, Rhys decided to help her win. After the final task, when Amarantha tortured her, he felt the mating bond snap into place. He picked up the dagger knowing he would die trying to save her—but Amarantha killed her first. As Feyre died, he held the bond and willed her to hold on, then entered every High Lord’s mind to persuade them to resurrect her. He almost told her about the bond the first time he summoned her Under the Moutain, but afterward convinced himself for three months she was safer without him. Yet he could feel her falling apart. When she begged for someone to stop the wedding, he went. When Tamlin locked her up, Rhys says he would have killed him if he’d been there. He didn’t tell her about the bond because he didn’t want to pressure her. And then he tells her he loves her.

Feyre gives him the soup—accepting the bond. She tells Rhys she loves him, that she is healing but every part of her heart belongs to him. They kiss and finally fully accept the mating bond.
